It is essentially me grabbing a story from the last 72 hours. I'm actually going to grab a story that happened about two and a half hours ago and try to turn that into a story for you. What's your last name? And I say, Dix. Now, Charlie and I are in Dix sporting goods. So she says to me, no, I don't need to know the name of the store. I need to know your last name. And I say, I know Dix. I teach Charlie later. You don't say my last name is Dix. You just keep saying Dix until it finally penetrates, which often takes a really long time. And with each utterance of the word Dix, you can see them short circuit. Even more than before. Now, sometimes when you say this, the person behind the counter will say to you, do you own the store? And that moment is fantastic. Because when they ask you that question, you get to explain what an apostrophe is and how this is not Dix Sporting Goods, the plural. You get to say that this is a guy named Dick or a guy whose last name is Dick, who owns a sporting goods store, but not my name does not have an apostrophe. I'm glad because it's not an easy name to carry, particularly early in life. Today, I don't care about my last name. Today, I kind of don't care about anything that anyone thinks, which is why I'm able to play golf poorly after 20 years. I don't care when I hit a bad shot, what my friends think. And I felt the change. And so it's often not a choice for me in terms of what the moral or the message or the meaning is going to be. It really is what I actually experienced is what I'm trying to say, which is why I tend not to use the word moral because a moral indicates a lesson. And oftentimes when I tell stories, people are learning lessons from me, but I am not ever trying to be explicit about it. I'm only trying to say what I have learned. And if they also want to simultaneously learn that that's great. Sometimes they disagree with me completely are but are still entertained. And at the end they're like, I think you were wrong, buddy. But that was entertaining. And I did see how you changed over time, right? Walter White in Breaking Bad is a great example of this. We don't really approve of the way Walter White changed over the course of Breaking Bad, right? He went from high school chemistry teacher to murderous, you know, drug dealer. But it was entertaining because it was changed over time, right? So there's no moral to that story necessarily, at least not for Walter White or the moral of the story is don't do that because it's going to end poorly for you. But I don't know if that would necessarily be a moral, but it is changed over time that we can disagree with and still be entertained by. So I don't really get to make those choices. I guess I'll say, because I can't answer any questions short in an easy way. I'll say that when I'm telling my students stories, I am sometimes telling them stories with the intention of them learning something more explicitly, but they're 10. And so for a 10 year old, I might finish that story and say what I'd like you to learn from that, you know, is this idea. And I would expound upon it almost like a TED talk, almost like a here's the story and here's the idea I have worth sharing. But if I'm just storytelling, I'm not going to actually try to teach anyone anything. If they want to learn, that's great. If they take something away from it and it changes the way they live their life, I think that's fantastic, but never necessary. My goal is to entertain only and everything else is a lovely additive. All that matters is the audience laugh. And if they laugh, they had fun that night, it was great. If they didn't, you got to take that lesson. I, I told a student on zoom, a student arrived to class on zoom and said, Oh, I'm sorry I'm late. My dog just pooped in the hallway. I'll pick it up though during the next break. And I said, go pick it up now. What are you some kind of a savage? And then after the workshop, this 23 year old woman named Lauren came on and said, Matt, I just wanted to alert you to the fact that the word savage was a word that was actually used to denigrate indigenous people and allow colonizers to murder them by characterizing them, not as human, but as savages. So it's a word we try to avoid these days. And I said, really? And I was really disappointed because it was a word I use all the damn time. And then I went and looked it up and she was unfortunately totally correct. It is absolutely a word used by white colonizers who wanted to murder indigenous people and rather than calling them human, they referred to the misavages. So I listened to those kinds of things. And then I listened to what resonates with people, because sometimes something that resonates with people and has universality, I will strengthen and bring more of it to a story, or dampen it if it doesn't strike people as like sort of universally touching them. So I listened to those things. But in the end, a lot of the notes I receive go by the wayside, because, you know, a lot of what my story is is what my story is. And I'm listening to the audience. I also believe that the most important audience for any story that we ever tell is ourselves. So we're telling it to ourselves first.
